<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:入湯税]][[ko:입탕세]]


{{Japanese law|qq=}}
[[File:草津温泉の湯畑03.jpg|thumb|Facilities of the {{ill|Yubatake|ja|湯畑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubatake|1=Yubatake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563768}} ({{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}}) maintained by the Nyūtōzei<ref name="mg">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://news.livedoor.com/article/detail/13690734/|author=飯塚玲児|authorlink=飯塚玲児|work={{ill|Magmag News|de|Magmag News|ja|まぐまぐ|zh|まぐまぐ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magmag News|1=Mag2|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11278197}}|date=2017-10-02|publisher={{ill|Livedoor News|de|Livedoor News|ja|ライブドアニュース|zh|ライブドアニュース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Livedoor News|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Livedoor ニュース|1=Livedoor News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11230327}}|title=Why is there a tax just for entering a hot spring? The “Nyūtōzei” that is forced to be paid at hot spring inns|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>]]
'''Nyūtōzei''' (入湯税) refers to a {{ill|Hypothecated tax|en|Hypothecated tax|qid=Q231826|lt=hypothecated tax|1=Hypothecated tax}} classified as a form of {{ill|Local tax|de|Kommunalsteuer|ja|地方税|zh|地方税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local tax|1=local tax|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3149547}}, which is imposed by a municipal government ({{ill|City, town, or village in Japan|de|Japanische Gemeinden|ja|市町村|zh|日本市町村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City, town, or village in Japan|1=city, town or village of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117351913}}) that has a {{ill|Mineral spring bathhouse|de|Mineralquellenbad|ja|鉱泉浴場|zh|矿泉浴场|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mineral spring bathhouse|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503427}} within its jurisdiction. The tax is levied on guests who enter such facilities. For small municipalities, it is a valuable source of independent revenue,{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=91|qq=}} yet, despite being a purpose tax, it is often used in a manner similar to general revenue.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=91|qq=}}<ref name="kk1909">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kankokeizai.com/%E3%80%90%E8%A6%B3%E5%85%89%E5%AD%A6%E3%81%B8%E3%81%AE%E3%83%8A%E3%83%93%E3%82%B2%E3%83%BC%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC-10%E3%80%91%E5%85%A5%E6%B9%AF%E7%A8%8E%E3%81%A8%E5%AE%BF%E6%B3%8A%E7%A8%8E%E3%81%AE/|author=高橋祐次|date=2019-09-03|title=[Navigator to Tourism Studies 10] Problems with Nyūtōzei and Lodging Tax as Tourism Promotion|publisher={{ill|Kankō Keizai Shimbun|de|Kankō Keizai Shimbun|ja|観光経済新聞|zh|観光経済新聞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankō Keizai Shimbun|1=Kankokeizai News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11630527}}|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>
{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}} According to the national standard tax rate set by Japan, the rate is 150 yen per person per day,{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}}<ref name="sm">{{Cite web|和書 |url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_content/000632884.pdf |title=Nyūtōzei |publisher={{ill|Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|en|Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|qid=Q1322293|lt=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications (Japan)|1=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications}} |accessdate=2021-05-21 |archive-url=https://warp.da.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/11518407/www.soumu.go.jp/main_content/000632884.pdf |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-12-09 |archive-date=2021-07-03 |language=ja|qq=}}</ref> and most municipalities follow this rate,<ref name="sm"/> though at the same time, most municipalities have exemptions in place.<ref name="rmc">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.rmc-chuo.jp/manager/2016092601.html|title=Industry/sector Topics: Ranking of Popular Onsen Viewed through Nyūtōzei (October 2016)|author=大野進一|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Small and Medium Enterprise Management Consultant Association|ja|東京都中小企業診断士協会|de|Tokyo SME Beraterverein|zh|東京都中小企業診断士協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Small and Medium Enterprise Management Consultant Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Central Branch|date=2016-10|accessdate=2021-05-17|qq=}}</ref> Conversely, some municipalities apply {{ill|Excess taxation|de|Überbesteuerung|ja=超過課税|zh=超過課税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Excess taxation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent source of revenue for tourism promotion.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=168|qq=}}
== Overview ==
Nyūtōzei is a {{ill|Tax|en|Tax|qid=Q8161|lt=Tax|1=Tax}} levied on hot spring ({{ill|Mineral spring|en|Mineral spring|qid=Q1365924|lt=Mineral spring|1=Mineral spring}}) bathers, for the stated purpose of funding the installation of environmental sanitation facilities, the maintenance of mineral spring sources, the strengthening of firefighting capabilities, and the promotion/development of tourism facilities.<ref name="sm"/> It has been designated as a purpose tax since 1957 (Showa 32).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=93|qq=}} Historically, it was introduced to provide a source of revenue for regions with few other taxable resources,{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|pp=91-92|qq=}} and while it accounted for only 0.1% of total municipal tax revenue nationwide in 2014 (Heisei 26),{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}} small municipalities see it as a critical source of self-generated revenue,{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=91|qq=}} especially in light of decreasing populations—and thus fewer payers of resident tax or corporate tax—and the potential for an increased number of non-resident visitors, including inbound tourists, who can contribute to growing Nyūtōzei revenue.{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}}
Nyūtōzei is stipulated in Articles 701 through 701-29 of the {{ill|Local Tax Act (Japan)|de|Lokale Steuergesetze in Japan|ja|地方税法|zh|日本地方税法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local Tax Act (Japan)|1=Local Tax Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424401}}.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=23|qq=}} The standard rate is 150 yen per person per day,{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}}<ref name="sm"/> {{sfn|藤中|2017|p=23|qq=}} but in practice, “1 overnight stay (1 night, 2 days)” is customarily counted as “1 day” for tax purposes.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=44|qq=}} Normally, when calculating a Japanese taxpayer’s tax obligation, any amount under 100 yen is cut off (rounded down), but Nyūtōzei does not undergo that rounding—if the tax is under 100 yen, it is still collected as is.<ref>{{ill|National Tax General Rules Act of Japan|de|National Tax General Rules Act of Japan|ja|国税通則法|zh|国税通則法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tax General Rules Act of Japan|1=Act on General Rules for National Taxes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11421208}} Article 119, paragraph (1)</ref><ref>Local Tax Act, Article 20-4-2, paragraph (3) main text</ref><ref>Same act, same article, same paragraph’s proviso; and Local Tax Act Enforcement Order Article 6-17 paragraph (2)</ref>
The taxpayer is the bather. However, in order to ease the collection process, the operator of the bathing facility (or another individual/entity who can conveniently collect the tax) is designated as a {{ill|Special collection|de|Besondere Erhebung|ja|特別徴収|zh|特別徴収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special collection|1=Special collection|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11570758}} agent under a municipal {{ill|By-law|en|By-law|qid=Q679847|lt=By-law|1=By-law}}.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=25|qq=}} In many cases, the bathing facility operator includes the Nyūtōzei in the bathing fee,<ref name="at">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://asahi.gr.jp/%E5%85%A5%E6%B9%AF%E7%A8%8E%E3%81%AB%E3%81%A4%E3%81%84%E3%81%A6%EF%BC%882015_2%E6%9C%88%E5%8F%B7%EF%BC%89/|title=About Nyūtōzei|author=中村勝宏|publisher={{ill|Asahi Tax Corporation|ja|税理士法人あさひ会計|de|Steuerkanzlei Asahi|zh|税理士法人あさひ会計|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Tax Corporation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|work=Asahi Newsletter February 2015|date=2015-02-12|accessdate=2021-05-18|qq=}}</ref> but whether or not the bathing tax is included in an overnight accommodation fee depends on the facility.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://faq.ikyu.com/app/answers/detail/a_id/155/~/%E3%80%90%E5%9B%BD%E5%86%85%E5%AE%BF%E6%B3%8A%E3%80%91%E5%85%A5%E6%B9%AF%E7%A8%8E%E3%81%AB%E3%81%A4%E3%81%84%E3%81%A6|title=[Domestic Accommodation] About Nyūtōzei|work={{ill|Ikyu.com|de|Ikyu.com|ja|一休 (企業)|zh|一休 (企業)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikyu.com|1=Ikyu Corporation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11352409}}|publisher={{ill|Ikyu (company)|de|Ikyu (company)|ja|一休 (企業)|zh|一休 (企業)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikyu (company)|1=Ikyu Corporation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11352409}}|accessdate=2021-05-18|qq=}}</ref> Even when accommodation fees are shown as tax-inclusive or paid electronically, some facilities require a separate payment of Nyūtōzei on-site.<ref name="mg"/> In the event Nyūtōzei is included in the accommodation fee, the following points apply:
# A travel coupon (ticket) issued on a tax-inclusive basis may include Nyūtōzei (along with other taxes).<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.tax.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/shitsumon/tozei/hotel/concern_b01.html|title=For Travel Agencies (Regarding coupon issuance)|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Government Bureau of Taxation|ja|東京都主税局|de|Finanzbehörde Tokio|zh|東京都主税局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Government Bureau of Taxation|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Government Bureau of Taxation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11525209}}|accessdate=2021-05-19|qq=}}</ref>
# Under the {{ill|Go To Travel campaign|ja|Go To キャンペーン|de|Go To Travel-Kampagne|zh|Go To トラベル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go To Travel campaign|1=Go To Campaign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q96602810}} discount program, if Nyūtōzei is included within the accommodation fees, it is also subject to the discount.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://faq.knt.co.jp/faq_detail.html?id=2876&PHPSESSID=ac5916bfb6f66152572354abd135d416|title=Is Nyūtōzei included in the Go To Travel Campaign discount?|publisher={{ill|Kinki Nippon Tourist|ja|近畿日本ツーリスト|de|Kinki Nippon Tourist|zh|近畿日本ツーリスト|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nippon Tourist|1=Kinki Nippon Tourist|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11638632}}|accessdate=2021-05-19|qq=}}</ref>
# If the accommodation fee (including Nyūtōzei) is recognized as a business expense, and the invoice or receipt explicitly shows Nyūtōzei, the portion relating to Nyūtōzei is a non-taxable purchase under the {{ill|Consumption tax (Japan)|de|japanische Verbrauchssteuer|ja|消費税 (日本)|zh|日本消费税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Consumption tax (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. If it is not shown, the entire accommodation cost counts as a taxable purchase.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://shouhizei-quiz.com/?p=7967|title=Accounting for Consumption Tax on Nyūtōzei in Hot Spring Accommodations|date=2020-12-14|author=川上悠季|publisher=Consumption Tax Q&A App Official Website|accessdate=2021-05-19|qq=}}</ref>
The special collection agent files a monthly tax return (covering from the 1st to the end of the month) by the 15th or the end of the following month,{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=25|qq=}} accompanied by the appropriate remittance. As a basis for filing, facilities must keep a ledger of daily bathers, bathing fees, and total Nyūtōzei collected; this ledger must be retained for at least one year.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=25|qq=}} Specific details can differ by municipality, since each municipality sets its own ordinances.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=25|qq=}}
Although the legal taxpayer is the bather and the hot spring operator is only a collecting agent, Nyūtōzei is usually classified as an {{ill|Indirect tax|de|Indirekte Steuer|ja|間接税|zh|间接税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Indirect tax|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 租税|1=Tax|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8161}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=[Tax System] Types and Classifications of Tax – Kids’ Tax Learning Corner|publisher={{ill|National Tax Agency|en|National Tax Agency|qid=Q11421205|lt=National Tax Agency (Japan)|1=National Tax Agency}}|url=https://www.nta.go.jp/taxes/kids/hatten/page02.htm|website=www.nta.go.jp|accessdate=2021-06-06|qq=}}</ref>
== Taxation and Legal Issues ==
Nyūtōzei is levied on the act of “entering a mineral spring bathhouse.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=23|qq=}} This definition raises multiple legal and practical questions; however, because the number of potential collectors (the special collection agents) is limited and the municipal tax authority maintains close communication with them, lawsuits rarely occur over this matter.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=28|qq=}}
=== Definition of Mineral Spring Bathhouse ===
The Local Tax Act does not define {{ill|Mineral spring bathhouse|de|Mineralquellenbad|ja|鉱泉浴場|zh|矿泉浴场|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mineral spring bathhouse|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503427}}. Court precedents have held that it should be based on the definition of an {{ill|Onsen (Hot springs law in Japan)|de|Onsen (Gesetz über heiße Quellen in Japan)|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|zh|日本温泉法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen (Hot springs law in Japan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}} “hot spring,”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=41|qq=}} but technical differences arise from the {{ill|Guidelines on Mineral Spring Analysis|de|Richtlinien zur Analyse von Mineralquellen|ja|鉱泉分析法指針|zh|鉱泉分析法指針|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guidelines on Mineral Spring Analysis|1=Guidelines for Mineral Spring Analysis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11649886}}. Accordingly, there are cases of “springs that qualify as onsen under the Onsen Act, but are not mineral springs,” or “springs that qualify as mineral springs but are not onsen,” such as cold springs.{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=41-42|qq=}} Further issues involve what counts as a “bathhouse,” e.g., does a foot bath or stone sauna constitute a bathhouse, or does a private tub in one’s room count?{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=42-43|qq=}} In practice, taxation is applied to facilities deemed to be mineral spring bathhouses “under general social perception.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=42|qq=}}
{{ill|Artificial Hot Springs|ja|人工温泉|1=Artificial Hot Spring|de|Künstliche Onsen|zh|人工温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Artificial Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Artificial Hot Spring|qq=}} are not considered onsen under the Onsen Act and thus technically fall outside the definition for Nyūtōzei,{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=41|qq=}} but because many municipalities consider them “mineral spring bathhouses” in a practical sense, they are sometimes still subject to Nyūtōzei.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://omotenashi.work/column/bits_of_knowledge/19438|title=Are Artificial Hot Springs Also Subject to Nyūtōzei?|work=Omotenashi HR|publisher={{ill|Nextbeat Co., Ltd.|ja|ネクストビート|de|Nextbeat|zh|ネクストビート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nextbeat Co., Ltd.|1=Nextbeat Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24867666}}|date=2021-02-10|accessdate=2021-05-22|qq=}}</ref> Likewise, facilities that transport hot spring water from outside municipal boundaries (so-called “transported hot water”) can also be taxed if they are recognized as mineral spring bathhouses.<ref>[https://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/zaisei/zeisei/qa/FAQ3263.html If I regularly fetch hot spring water from outside the city and heat it for business, do I have to collect Nyūtōzei from users?] {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka City|1=Fukuoka}} Asset Taxation Division, 2018-01-04 (accessed 2023-01-25).</ref>
=== Definition of the Act of Bathing ===
Because Nyūtōzei is defined as a tax on the act of bathing in a mineral spring (an “act tax”), it should, in theory, apply to every instance of bathing, regardless of the type of facility or whether the bather is lodging there.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=43|qq=}} However, the rationale for taxing the act of bathing is that it often involves “luxury expenditure” such as lodging, dining, and recreation.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=43|qq=}} Therefore, municipal ordinances often grant reductions or exemptions for uses that are not deemed luxury (e.g., communal or general public baths, or bathing for medical treatment).{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=43|qq=}} Another issue is that purely free bathing (i.e., zero monetary outlay) falls outside the definition of a taxable “expenditure,” even though it may still be an “act of bathing.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=44|qq=}}
Interpreted literally, “bathing” means physically entering a hot spring tub, yet there may be people who enter the facility without actually bathing in the mineral spring, or who only use non-mineral water facilities. In practice, it is impossible to check each person’s actual bathing behavior.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=43|qq=}} For example, in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|qid=Q526760|lt=Tonami, Toyama|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, even if a visitor does not actually bathe, they must pay Nyūtōzei unless they can prove they did not bathe in the hot spring.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.tonami.toyama.jp/faq/1300954468.html|title=Do we have to pay the tax if we only used a meeting room in a hot spring facility without bathing?|work=FAQ|date=2011-03-24|publisher=Tonami City Tax Division|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref> On the other hand, for a case where someone was billed for Nyūtōzei for days they did not bathe, attorney Tetsu Okumura responded on {{ill|Bengo4.com|ja|弁護士ドットコム|de|Bengo4.com|zh|弁護士ドットコム|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengo4.com|1=Bengo4.com|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27016929}} that “I believe one does not have to pay for days in which one actually did not bathe.”<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bengo4.com/c_15/b_535315/|title=Question about Nyūtōzei while traveling|work=Bengo4.com Tax Litigation|date=2017-03-21|publisher=Bengo4.com|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>
Related to this, in a legal case where a municipality insisted that every person entering a large complex with a hot spring facility be counted as a “bather,” the operator objected. The {{ill|Osaka High Court|ja|大阪高等裁判所|de|Obergericht Osaka|zh|大阪高等裁判所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka High Court|1=Ōsaka High Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q74996}} ultimately ruled that if the operator does not keep an exact count of bathers, the municipal authority must estimate the bather count by rational calculation based on the available evidence.{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=31-39|qq=}} The same judgment found that “people who bathe in tubs with no mineral spring water do not fall under the act of bathing in a mineral spring.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=44|qq=}}
== History ==
The origins of Nyūtōzei trace back to the “miscellaneous tax” ({{ill|Zasshūzei|ja|雑種税|de|Zasshūzei|zh|雑種税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasshūzei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) established under the {{ill|1879 Local Tax Regulations|ja|地方税規則 (明治11年)|de|Lokalsteuerverordnung 1879|zh|地方税規則 (明治11年)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1879 Local Tax Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1879 (Meiji 12).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92|qq=}} Back then, “miscellaneous tax” was one of several taxes that prefectures could collect, applying to small-scale businesses that were not covered by the {{ill|Business tax (Japan)|ja|営業税 (日本)|de|Gewerbesteuer (Japan)|zh|日本营业税|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Business tax (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 営業税|1=business tax|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3796969}}. Public bathhouses (“yuyas”) fell under that scope, but these corresponded more closely to today’s {{ill|Sentō|en|Sentō|qid=Q1046648|lt=Sento|1=Sentō}} rather than onsen.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92|qq=}} It was not until 1927 (Showa 2) that miscellaneous tax could be applied to onsen. In 1940 (Showa 15), the Local Tax Act abolished prefectural business tax and miscellaneous tax,{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92|qq=}} but municipalities that had already been imposing “Nyūtōzei” (tax on hot spring bathers) or “Kōsenzei” (tax on hot spring bathhouse owners) before the law’s enactment were allowed to continue.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92, 100|qq=}}
In 1947 (Showa 22), the Local Tax Act designated a “Nyūtōzei” for prefectures to impose on hot spring bathers, with a provision allowing municipalities to add a surtax.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92|qq=}} At that time, it was still a purpose tax,{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=27|qq=}} but under the current Local Tax Act established on September 13, 1950 (Showa 25),{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}} Nyūtōzei became a “statutory ordinary tax” at the municipal level.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=27|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=92|qq=}} On April 1, 1957 (Showa 32),{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}} it was changed back to a purpose tax “to be allocated for the maintenance of environmental sanitation and other tourism facilities,”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=24|qq=}} with subsequent revisions adding “firefighting facilities” in 1971 (Showa 46),{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=93|qq=}}{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=24|qq=}} “protection/maintenance of mineral spring sources” in 1977 (Showa 52),{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=24-25|qq=}} and “tourism promotion” in 1990 (Heisei 2).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=93|qq=}} Because costs for onsen facility development could not be included in {{ill|Local Allocation Tax Grant|ja|地方交付税交付金|de|Lokaler Zuweisungsteuerzuschuss|zh|地方交付税交付金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local Allocation Tax Grant|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地方交付税|1=local allocation tax|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424339}}, local governments used Nyūtōzei for such purposes.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=24|qq=}}
During the {{ill|2004 Hot springs labeling scandal|ja|2004年の温泉偽装問題|de|Onsen-Etikettenschwindel 2004|zh|2004年温泉虚假标示问题|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2004 Hot springs labeling scandal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it became public that some bathhouses that were not using actual mineral springs still collected Nyūtōzei. In other cases, certain local authorities were found to have inappropriately exempted some facilities. <ref>[http://mainichi.jp/area/news/20120920ddn012040060000c.html Disproportionate Filings: A Public Bath in Amagasaki, Hyōgo – the City’s Improper Approval for Reduced Nyūtōzei], Mainichi Shimbun, September 20, 2012</ref>
=== Changes in the Standard Tax Rate ===
* 1950 – 10 yen{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}}{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}}
* 1953 – 20 yen{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}}
* 1971 – 40 yen{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}}{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}}
* 1975 – 100 yen{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020b|p=11|qq=}}
* 1978 – 150 yen{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}}
== Adoption Status of the Tax Rate ==
Although 150 yen per day is merely the statutory “standard tax rate,”{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}} municipalities can set it according to their needs.{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=23-24|qq=}} As of fiscal 2018 (Heisei 30), the lowest rate is 20 yen and the highest is 250 yen.<ref name="sm"/> 992 municipalities (counting Tokyo’s 23 wards as one) impose Nyūtōzei, and 91.3% adopt the standard 150 yen.<ref name="sm"/>

These are just the “standard” rates set by each municipality,<ref name="sm"/> and in fact, most provide various exemptions.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=45|qq=}}<ref name="rmc"/> For example, some charge 100 yen for day-use at a {{ill|Super Sentō|lt=Super Sentō|lt=Super sento|ja|スーパー銭湯|de|Super-Sento|zh|超级钱汤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Super sento|qq=}},<ref name="rmc"/> or exempt extended-stay medical sojourns, school trip participants,<ref name="rmc"/> children under 12, and those using communal or general public bathhouses<ref name="at"/> from Nyūtōzei.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=43|qq=}} Certain municipalities, such as {{ill|Kusatsu, Gunma|en|Kusatsu, Gunma|qid=Q1358949|lt=Kusatsu, Gunma|1=Kusatsu, Gunma}} ({{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}}) and {{ill|Yufu|en|Yufu|qid=Q990455|lt=Yufu, Ōita|1=Yufu}} ({{ill|Yufuin Onsen|de|Yufuin Onsen|ja|湯布院温泉|zh|湯布院温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuin Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯布院温泉郷|1=Yufuin Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q86732501}}), use a sliding scale based on accommodation cost.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=93|qq=}} Meanwhile, {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}} introduced a lodging tax on April 1, 2020, and simultaneously reduced Nyūtōzei from 150 yen to 50 yen for overnight stays “while the lodging tax is in effect.”<ref>[https://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/zaisei/zeisei/life/006.html About Nyūtōzei] Fukuoka City, 2022-06-10 (accessed 2023-01-25).</ref>
Some municipalities have no ordinance at all for Nyūtōzei, even though they technically have mineral spring facilities within their boundaries.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=40|qq=}} Under a strict reading of the Local Tax Act, “if you have a mineral spring bathhouse, you must impose Nyūtōzei,” yet certain municipalities collect it unofficially through administrative “guidelines.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=40-41|qq=}} Legal scholar Toshihiro Fujinaka notes that, generally speaking, “Without an ordinance, even long-standing customs of collection cannot stand, and collecting under mere guidelines is not legally recognized.”{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=41|qq=}}
=== Excess Taxation ===
When a local government sets the Nyūtōzei rate above the standard 150 yen, it is called “excess taxation.”{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}} As of fiscal 2012 (Heisei 24), only two municipalities—{{ill|Kuwana, Mie|en|Kuwana, Mie|qid=Q496472|lt=Kuwana, Mie|1=Kuwana, Mie}} ({{ill|Nagashima Onsen|ja|長島温泉|de|Nagashima Onsen|zh|长岛温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Onsen|1=Nagashima Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11652287}}) and {{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|qid=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka, Okayama|1=Mimasaka, Okayama}} ({{ill|Yunogo Onsen|en|Yunogo Onsen|qid=Q8061351|lt=Yunogo Onsen|1=Yunogo Onsen}})—had done so,{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=93|qq=}} until {{ill|Kushiro|en|Kushiro|qid=Q271917|lt=Kushiro, Hokkaido|1=Kushiro}} ({{ill|Akanko Onsen|ja|阿寒湖温泉|de|Akanko Onsen|zh|阿寒湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akanko Onsen|1=Akanko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657382}}) began imposing it in fiscal 2015 (Heisei 27).{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=171|qq=}} Kuwana and Mimasaka were continuing the old rates from their former municipalities under a municipal merger arrangement, while Kushiro used the “unequal tax rate” provision of the Local Tax Act—hence a difference in legal basis.{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=44-45|qq=}} In recent years, {{ill|Minoh|en|Minoh|qid=Q665097|lt=Minoh, Osaka|1=Minoh}} ({{ill|Minoh Onsen|ja|箕面温泉|de|Minoh Onsen|zh|箕面温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minoh Onsen|1=Minoh Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11603213}}) and {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|qid=Q273880|lt=Beppu, Ōita|1=Beppu}} ({{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|qid=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen}}) have also raised their Nyūtōzei rates,{{sfn|池田|2019|p=266, 269|qq=}} meaning five municipalities had introduced excess taxation by fiscal 2018. <ref name="sm"/> Because Nyūtōzei mostly affects non-resident visitors, who are easier to tax from an administrative perspective,{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=49|qq=}} some municipalities have considered positioning Nyūtōzei as a “tourist tax.”{{sfn|合田・バロン・三友|2018|p=78|qq=}} However, small onsen inns and facilities that mainly serve local residents, such as certain {{ill|Super Sentō|lt=Super Sentō|lt=Super sento|ja|スーパー銭湯|de|Super-Sento|zh|超级钱汤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Super sento|qq=}}, may resist any rate hike, as they cannot readily pass on the extra cost.{{sfn|藤中|2017|pp=49-50|qq=}}
In 1963 (Showa 38), {{ill|Nagashima Onsen|ja|長島温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Onsen|1=Nagashima Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11652287}} was discovered in the former {{ill|Nagashima, Mie|en|Nagashima, Mie|qid=Q6958641|lt=Nagashima, Mie (town)|1=Nagashima, Mie}}. The town introduced Nyūtōzei at 20 yen in 1964 (Showa 39).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=96|qq=}} Over time, more lodging facilities opened, and Nyūtōzei eventually had three different rates for day-use, dormitories/retreats, and hotels, with only hotels paying a surcharge.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=96|qq=}} Following the 2004 (Heisei 16) merger of Nagashima, Kuwana City, and Tado Town into a new “Kuwana City,” the tax rates were unified under the old Nagashima Town scheme five years post-merger.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=96|qq=}} As of 2015, only four hotels (three in old Nagashima) actually pay the excess portion.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=96|qq=}} Unlike other municipalities that use the extra revenue for special tourism programs, Kuwana does not implement any particular measures for using it.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=49|qq=}}
In Yunogo Onsen, the old {{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama (town)|ja|美作町 (岡山県)|de|Mimasaka (ehemalige Stadt in Okayama)|zh|美作町 (岡山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimasaka, Okayama (town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} had a separate “bathing fee” (50 yen) on top of the 150-yen Nyūtōzei, but since that caused confusion regarding its usage, the two were combined at a 200-yen Nyūtōzei.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=94|qq=}} When Mimasaka Town merged into Mimasaka City in 2005 (Heisei 17), the new city unified Nyūtōzei at 200 yen.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=95|qq=}} Mimasaka City returns half of what it collects in Nyūtōzei to local inns’ associations for tourism promotion.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|pp=94-95|qq=}}
From April 2019 (Heisei 31) in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|qid=Q273880|lt=Beppu, Ōita|1=Beppu}}, Nyūtōzei became linked to lodging and dining expenses, resulting in a 144.37 million yen increase (a 45% rise) over the prior year.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.travelnews.co.jp/news/area/2021051915270121988.html|title=Japan Onsen Association Releases Top 30 Municipalities in 2019 Nyūtōzei Revenue|work=Travel News at|date=2021-05-19|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>
==== Example of Kushiro City ====
{{ill|Akanko Onsen|ja|阿寒湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akanko Onsen|1=Akanko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657382}} in {{ill|Kushiro|en|Kushiro|qid=Q271917|lt=Kushiro, Hokkaido|1=Kushiro}} drew 1.695 million tourists at its peak in 2002 (Heisei 14), but that fell to 967 thousand by 2013 (Heisei 25).{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=170|qq=}} Thus, in 2013, the local ryokan association unanimously resolved to seek a higher Nyūtōzei, and the local tourism promotion group established an “Independent Revenue Study Panel,”{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=170|qq=}} partnering with the Tourism Policy Research Department of the {{ill|Japan Travel Bureau Foundation|ja|日本交通公社 (公益財団法人)|de|Japan Travel Bureau Stiftung|zh|日本交通公社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Travel Bureau Foundation|1=Japan Travel Bureau Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11507170}} to survey visitors’ willingness to pay.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=91, 97|qq=}} Over half of respondents indicated they would “cooperate financially if usage is made transparent” (50.7%) or “are open to cooperating with local communities” (18.5%). The most commonly acceptable extra tax amounts were “151–200 yen” (30.1%) and “101–150 yen” (21.6%).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|pp=98-99|qq=}}{{sfn|高橋|2017|pp=170-171|qq=}}
Based on this, the region submitted an excess taxation proposal to Kushiro City, which approved raising Nyūtōzei by 100 yen (to 250 yen) for 10 years starting in fiscal 2015.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=171|qq=}} Because many tourists only supported the increase if the usage was strictly for tourism promotion, the extra portion was placed in the “Kushiro City Tourism Promotion Temporary Fund,” with disbursements strictly limited to tourism-related initiatives.{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}}{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=172|qq=}}
Despite worries that visitor numbers might drop, lodging in Akanko Onsen actually rose from 1.29 million guests in FY2014 to 1.45 million in FY2016 (an increase by 1.12x), while Nyūtōzei revenue jumped from 108 million yen to 157 million yen (1.45x).{{sfn|池田|2019|p=268|qq=}} By contrast, {{ill|Tōkamachi|en|Tōkamachi|qid=Q858369|lt=Tōkamachi, Niigata|1=Tōkamachi}}, which raised its Nyūtōzei from 100 yen to 150 yen, suffered a drop in overnight stays of 45 thousand (about 16%), although its tax revenue did rise by 1.69 times.{{sfn|池田|2019|pp=269-270|qq=}}
==== Example of Nasushiobara City ====
In {{ill|Nasushiobara|en|Nasushiobara|qid=Q498027|lt=Nasushiobara, Tochigi|1=Nasushiobara}}, Mayor Michitaro Watanabe proposed monthly {{ill|PCR test|ja|PCR検査|de|PCR-Test|zh|PCR检验|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/PCR test|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ポリメラーゼ連鎖反応|1=Polymerase chain reaction|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q176996}}s for about 600 tourism employees to demonstrate thorough COVID-19 measures, funding part of it by raising Nyūtōzei by up to 200 yen.<ref name="ntv2009">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://news.ntv.co.jp/category/society/726716|title="A Mayor’s Secret Strategy" Divides a Hot Spring Town|date=2020-09-22|work=NTV News 24|publisher={{ill|Nippon Television|en|Nippon Television|qid=Q864910|lt=Nippon TV|1=Nippon Television}}|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref> While many businesses in {{ill|Itamuro Onsen|ja|板室温泉|de|Itamuro Onsen|zh|板室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itamuro Onsen|1=Itamuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11532586}} supported this due to severe revenue losses during the pandemic, most owners in {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉|de|Shiobara Onsen|zh|塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩原温泉郷|1=Shiobara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428634}} opposed it.<ref name="ntv2009"/> Ultimately, a compromise was reached, setting three tiers of increases<ref name="nhk2009">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/news/html/20200928/k10012638321000.html|title=Raising Nyūtōzei to Fund Regular PCR Tests for Hotel Workers, Nasushiobara, Tochigi|work=NHK NEWS WEB|publisher={{ill|NHK|en|NHK|qid=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}}|date=2020-09-28|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref> rather than a flat 200 yen, and the city council approved it.<ref name="ntv2009"/> The new rate began December 1, 2020 (Reiwa 2).<ref name="nhk2012">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/news/html/20201202/k10012741291000.html|title=Nyūtōzei Raised in Nasushiobara to Fund PCR Testing|work=NHK NEWS WEB|publisher=NHK|date=2020-12-02|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>
Though the city had hoped for around 600 monthly PCR tests, only 63 tests were conducted in total by late November 2020. To encourage more participation, the city made tests free for hotel/ryokan employees and enabled mail-in or city-pickup of samples.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/397201|title=From January, PCR Tests Will Be Free for Accommodation Workers, 1,000 Yen for Local Residents and Elderly Facilities; Nasushiobara City|publisher=[[:en:Shimotsuke Shinbun|Shimotsuke Shimbun]]|date=2020-12-17|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref> The measure was initially planned through March 31, 2022 (Reiwa 4), but ended early on September 30, 2021 (Reiwa 3).<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.nasushiobara.lg.jp/soshikikarasagasu/kazeika/oshirase/5887.html|title=Regarding the End of the Raised Nyūtōzei Period|date=2021-11-30|publisher=Nasushiobara City Tax Division|accessdate=2022-05-05|qq=}}</ref>
According to city officials, this project gained recognition—such as being selected in the world’s Top 100 Sustainable Tourism Destinations—and they deemed it a success, although the tax revenue was below the original 80 million yen annual projection, and only 27 out of 82 inns actually participated in the PCR testing. Some participants felt that monthly tests alone could not fully guarantee safety.<ref name="ah2112">{{cite web|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASPDW755SPDRUUHB001.html|title=Did the “Raise Nyūtōzei to Fund Staff PCR Tests” Plan in Nasushiobara Succeed?|publisher=Asahi Shimbun|author=小野智美|date=2021-12-28|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref>
== Municipalities with High Nyūtōzei Revenue ==
Because most local governments adopt the standard rate of 150 yen, the scale of Nyūtōzei revenue often reflects the popularity of a given {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} region.{{refn|group="注"|Some organizations, such as the Tokyo SME Management Consultant Association Central Branch<ref name="rmc" /> and the Ministry of the Environment’s Working Group on Onsen Revitalization,<ref name="env">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/council/yuushikisya/02yuushikisya/sanko2.pdf|date=2017-06-29|title=Basic Data on Onsen Areas|work=Reference Material 3, 2nd Meeting of the Working Group on Onsen Revitalization|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|accessdate=2021-05-17|qq=}}</ref> highlight top Nyūtōzei earners as “popular onsen areas.”|qq=}} Below are the top 10 municipalities in FY2019 (Heisei 31/Reiwa 1).<ref name="spa2">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/onsen_wp/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/nyuutouzei_best30_2019.pdf|title=Top 30 Municipalities in Nyūtōzei Revenue (2019)|publisher={{ill|Japan Spa Association|ja|日本温泉協会|de|Japan Spa Association|zh|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Spa Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60987269}}|date=2021-05-17|accessdate=2021-05-21|qq=}}</ref> Out of total Nyūtōzei revenue nationwide (22.49773 billion yen), these 10 account for 16.4%.<ref name="spa2" />

By prefecture, Hokkaido Ranked first in FY2016 (Heisei 28) with 2.423 billion yen, followed by Shizuoka (1.694 billion), Nagano (1.270 billion), Kanagawa (0.952 billion), and Gunma (0.899 billion).{{sfn|池田|2019|p=250|qq=}} The prefecture with the smallest total Nyūtōzei revenue was Nara (0.041 billion).{{sfn|池田|2019|p=250|qq=}} As for the ratio of Nyūtōzei to total local tax revenue, Yamanashi was the highest at over 0.3%, followed by Akita, Nagano, Yamagata, and Ōita; the lowest was Saitama.{{sfn|池田|2019|p=251|qq=}}
=== Long-Term Statistics ===
In 1986 (Showa 61), the Ranking of total Nyūtōzei revenue shifted, with Hakone surpassing Atami.{{sfn|熱海市観光建設部観光経済課|2020a|p=21|qq=}}
(For details of year-by-year top earners, see references in the original text.)
== Usage ==
Nyūtōzei may be used for four specified purposes under the Local Tax Act:{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=169|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=91|qq=}}<ref name="kk1909"/>
1. Installation of environmental sanitation facilities  
2. Installation of facilities for protecting/managing mineral spring sources  
3. Installation of firefighting facilities and related resources  
4. Promotion of tourism (including tourism facility development)  
In practice, each municipality allocates the tax revenue differently,{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=49|qq=}}{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=170|qq=}} often folding it into the general budget and assigning some portion to tourism. Some municipalities clearly report the usage, while many do not disclose detailed breakdowns, leading to criticism that Nyūtōzei effectively becomes general revenue. <ref name="kk1909" /> With infrastructure largely improved compared to decades past, debate continues over how strictly the revenue should be tied to onsen-related facilities.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=27, 49|qq=}}{{sfn|合田・バロン・三友|2018|p=78|qq=}}
In {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|qid=Q819639|lt=Gero, Gifu|1=Gero, Gifu}} ({{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|de|Gero Onsen|zh|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}), for example, annual Nyūtōzei revenue of 150 million yen is split into 50 million for a fund reserve and 100 million for tourism promotion.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=170|qq=}} In {{ill|Hakone|en|Hakone|qid=Q671040|lt=Hakone, Kanagawa|1=Hakone}}, which collected 684.7 million yen in FY2016, about 60.1% went to environmental sanitation, 25.2% to tourism promotion (marketing, PR), 12.3% to tourism facilities, and 2.3% to firefighting. In {{ill|Kusatsu, Gunma|en|Kusatsu, Gunma|qid=Q1358949|lt=Kusatsu, Gunma|1=Kusatsu, Gunma}}, which collected 226.66 million yen, about 44.6% funded improvements around the {{ill|Yubatake|ja|湯畑|1=Yubatake|2=ja|3=湯畑|4=zh|5=汤畑|6=de|7=Onsen-Felder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubatake|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubatake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563768}} and {{ill|Sainokawara Park|ja|西の河原|de|Sainokawara Park|zh|西之河原公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sainokawara Park|1=Sainokawara Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11627049}}, 15.4% went to the local tourism association, and 5.7% to events/advertising.<ref name="mg"/> Meanwhile, {{ill|Atami|en|Atami|qid=Q653364|lt=Atami, Shizuoka|1=Atami}} uses 51% for tourism, and {{ill|Sapporo|en|Sapporo|qid=Q37951|lt=Sapporo|1=Sapporo}} uses 68% for tourism, with little or none spent on environmental sanitation.{{sfn|藤中|2017|p=49|qq=}}
In some municipalities, especially after mergers, confusion arises over whether revenue from onsen areas might be spent on projects unrelated to those onsen, prompting dissatisfaction from lodging operators who bear the special collection burden. <ref name="kk1909"/> Some also question the fairness of treating onsen visitors as a separate tax base for general tourism development. <ref name="kk1909"/> The Onsen Machidukuri Research Group proposed in May 2011 (Heisei 23) that municipalities should disclose Nyūtōzei revenue/usage to facilitate better onsen-centered community development.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=170|qq=}}
=== Example of Toba City ===
Although {{ill|Toba, Mie|en|Toba, Mie|qid=Q819629|lt=Toba, Mie|1=Toba, Mie}} has some onsen accommodations,{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=95|qq=}}{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=173|qq=}} it previously had no Nyūtōzei ordinance, partly because the city did not own or directly manage its hot spring sources,{{sfn|梅川ほか 編|2014|p=9|qq=}} and partly because many facilities used transported water.{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=95|qq=}} But after discussions with private onsen operators, Toba City decided to adopt Nyūtōzei in April 2007 (Heisei 19).{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=95|qq=}} Its revenue was zero until FY2006,{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=173|qq=}} yet by FY2019 it reached 173,627,000 yen—17th nationwide (the highest in Mie Prefecture).<ref name="spa2"/>
Toba enacted the “Toba City Kōsengen Hoho-Kanri Seibi Hojo Kingyō Kisoku” (Regulations for Subsidies to Preserve and Manage Mineral Springs), allocating 50% of Nyūtōzei to tourism promotion, 30% to protecting mineral springs, and 10% each to firefighting and environmental sanitation.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=173|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|pp=95-96|qq=}} The portion for mineral spring protection is provided as a subsidy to the Toba Onsen Promotion Association, while the remaining 70% is put into a Tourism Promotion Fund, which finances projects named in the city’s tourism master plan or action programs, under the management of the fiscal division.{{sfn|梅川ほか 編|2014|p=10|qq=}}
With a dedicated fund, Toba can carry over unspent money to future fiscal years, avoiding the pitfalls of strict annual budgeting.{{sfn|高橋|2017|p=173|qq=}}{{sfn|梅川・吉澤・福永|2015|p=96|qq=}} For instance, after the Great East Japan Earthquake in March 2011, Toba quickly mounted a tourism campaign using those reserves.{{sfn|梅川ほか 編|2014|p=10|qq=}} This fund also led to more staff and bigger tourism budgets. Nonetheless, city officials recognize that because the fund’s flexibility is high, they must be accountable for its results.{{sfn|梅川ほか 編|2014|p=10|qq=}}
